courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Introductory Humanities: Great Books
courses.description: An introduction to the humanities through the topic of “Great Books.” Students will closely analyze primary canonical texts from the West and from the global south. Political, religious, philosophical, and literary works by important writers and communities from across the globe will be explored to understand the human condition and self-formation. Such engagement with primary texts will be put into the context of larger topics such as class, race, and gender in addition to colonialism, decolonialism, and postcolonial modernity.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Virginia Tech Prison Book Project
courses.description: A one-hour course with the Virginia Tech Prison Book Project. Students will complete a learning module about carceral institutions in the United States and the impact of educational opportunities on the lives of incarcerated people. They will then participate in a service learning event where they match individual requests from incarcerated readers to books and prepare the books for shipping.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: The Culture of Money
courses.description: Exploration of the cultural, religious, and ethical dimensions of money and related topics, such as gifting, markets, the charging of interest, charity, pricing strategies, private property, and wage labor. Comparison of the disparate forms and uses of money and how money has worked in various cultural and historical frameworks. Evaluation of the differences between capitalist and non-capitalist money-systems, including philosophical debates about the relationship between money and human nature. Examination of ethical issues pertaining to money, such as the forgiveness of debt, money’s impact on social inequality, and the role of money in various projects of political empowerment and disempowerment.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Ghosts
courses.description: Fundamental humanities and social scientific frameworks underlying belief in ghosts and the paranormal as sites of human meaning-making and as expressions of cultural norms and anxieties concerning human behavior and social relationships, especially in US contexts pertaining to enslavement, displacement, and gender disparity. Introduces students to key concepts and methods such as auto-ethnography, the study of material culture, historical criticism, and literary criticism via an appealing and engaging topic. Training in ethical self-assessment and assessment of human meaning-making and behavior concerning ghosts and the paranormal such as affect theory, empiricism, and experience.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Introductory Humanities: The Modern World
courses.description: The shifts in thought and values during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries in the global imagination, including issues of commerce, scientific inquiry, industrialization, nationalism, war, labor, gender, class differences, race, and the beginnings of postmodernity. Emphasis on interpretive and analytic skills in terms of reading, discussing, and writing about the interrelationships among the arts, literature, philosophy, history, religion, and science, and their contributions toward shaping the values and aspirations of the age, including global contexts and Asian cultures.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Introduction to Popular Culture
courses.description: The development and formation of the category of popular culture. Competing theories and methods for analyzing popular culture. Activities, objects, and ideas included under the rubric of popular culture. Critical thinking about the production of popular culture in relation to race, gender, class, and other forms of human difference.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Introduction to Humanities and the Arts
courses.description: Explores the written, visual, and performing arts of selected periods and cultures, setting them in the context of their times. Study of these periods linked with overarching questions of cultural encounters, interactions, and negotiations. Introduces principles of each art form as well as the means of appreciation. Students taught methods in researching, writing, and presenting on these art forms.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Introduction to Appalachian Studies
courses.description: Introduces students to the history of the Appalachian region from European contact to the present. Traces the idea of Appalachia by tracing ways in which Americans have imagined the region over time. Explores humanistic problems of cultural identity, race and ethnicity, place and globalization, and impacts of natural resource extraction.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Oral Traditions and Culture
courses.description: Examination of the worlds great oral traditions, both ancient and contemporary. Emphasis on performance contexts, relationships among multicultural traditions, including American Indian oral traditions, and the relationships among orality, literacy, technology, media, and culture.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: The Creative Process
courses.description: Explores ways in which creativity and design can be understood historically as well as understood and practiced in a classroom setting. Subjects include any or all of the following: theories of creativity; traditions associated with understanding and making several kinds of art; studying artworks from different cultural backgrounds, working with the limitations and possibilities inherent in design projects, and examining how and why they were created; and preparing final creative projects for classroom presentation.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Campus Free Speech
courses.description: Historical, religious, philosophical, and legal frameworks that shape modern debates on campus free speech and academic freedom. Focusing on case studies this course offers a non-partisan examination of hate speech codes, speaker shout-downs, ideological diversity, university neutrality, the limits of academic freedom, and other issues relevant to students, faculty, and administrators at public and private US universities. Students will engage in practical training in civil and respectful deliberation about matters of law and policy, the pursuit of truth, and the pursuit of permissive versus censorious approaches to campus speech.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Folk Cultures in Appalachia
courses.description: Examination of the expressive genres and cultural processes of communities in Appalachia. Documentation of art and skill in everyday life, including material culture (e.g., foodways, architecture), customary behavior (e.g., music, ritual, occupational practice), and verbal art (e.g., narrative, speechplay), and analysis of how people have used these forms to shape social identities, physical spaces, and power relations.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Introduction to American Studies
courses.description: Methodology and tools of American studies, with a focus on developing analytic skills to assess discourse across varied media. Interdisciplinary investigation of histories, politics, cultures, and beliefs in the Americas, including the impacts of encounter and exchange. Intensive study of a specific topic or period.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Theories of Popular Culture
courses.description: Examination of theories for understanding the ways in which popular objects and practices (such as television programs, films, or attending sporting events) represent, maintain, and contest societal norms, including norms regarding gender and sexuality, race and ethnicity, and class and place, with an emphasis on the United States.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Theories of Popular Culture
courses.description: Examination of theories for understanding the ways in which popular objects and practices (such as television programs, films, or attending sporting events) represent, maintain, and contest societal norms, including norms regarding gender and sexuality, race and ethnicity, and class and place, with an emphasis on the United States.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Multicultural Communication
courses.description: Exploration of communication in and among various cultural groups through an examination of communicative practices, registers, discourse, and performance. Emphasis on understanding cultural differences and similarities in the different styles and stances in communication and their meanings to participants.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Multicultural Communication
courses.description: Exploration of communication in and among various cultural groups through an examination of communicative practices, registers, discourse, and performance. Emphasis on understanding cultural differences and similarities in the different styles and stances in communication and their meanings to participants.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Appalachian Communities
courses.description: The concept of community in Appalachia using an interdisciplinary approach and experiential learning. Interrelationships among geographically, culturally, and socially constituted communities, public policy, and human development. Pre: Junior standing.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Explorations in Advanced Humanities Topics
courses.description: In-depth study of special interdisciplinary topic. Topics vary but involve a close and extensive study of the interrelationship between cultural ideas and their expressions in several of the following forms: literature, philosophy, religion, art, music, drama, material culture, and popular culture. May be repeated with different topics, for a maximum of 9 credits.

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Topics in Culture
courses.description: Uses sociological, anthropological, as well as artistic and humanist paradigms to analyze culture. Discusses 20th and 21st century cultural trends. Analyzes the implications of social context for cultural artifacts such as art. Topics are variable. Example topics include the cultural construction of race and the cultural of the nineteen sixties. Course may be repeated with different course content for up to 6 credits. Pre: Junior or Senior standing.

prerequisites.rawText: SOC 1004 or SOC 1014 or AFST 1714 or AINS 1104 or RLCL 1004 or RLCL 2004 or WGS 1824

courses.subject: HUM
courses.title: Issues in Appalachian Studies
courses.description: Research conducted by students on issues relevant to local or regional sustainability in contemporary Appalachia on contemporary environmental and community issues. Focus on environmental justice ethical issues expressed in or created by various forms of discourse.
